sections.heading: Licensing Roles & Entities
content: Principal Auctioneer: An individual who, for a fee or commission, offers and executes a listing contract, sale, purchase, or exchange of goods, and is responsible for the management and supervision of an auction company.
Affiliate Auctioneer: An individual who is employed, directly or indirectly, by a principal auctioneer to engage in auction activities for compensation.
Bid Caller Auctioneer: An individual hired by a principal auctioneer, public automobile auction, or public automobile auctioneer strictly to solicit bids for the purchase of goods at an auction.
Public Automobile Auctioneer: An individual who offers or negotiates a listing contract, sale, or exchange of motor vehicles at auction.
Ringperson (Ringman): An individual who assists the auctioneer on the floor by spotting bids in the audience and relaying them, but who does not vocally conduct the auction themselves.
Commission: The five-member Tennessee Auctioneer Commission, which regulates and licenses auctioneers in the state.

sections.heading: Auction Types & Bidding Terminology
content: Auction: A sales transaction conducted by oral, written, or electronic exchange consisting of a series of invitations by the auctioneer for offers, culminating in the acceptance of the highest or most favorable offer.

With Reserve: An auction where the auctioneer retains the right to withdraw the goods at any time until announcing completion of the sale. Under the UCC, all auctions are presumed to be "with reserve" unless explicitly stated otherwise.
Without Reserve (Absolute Auction): An auction where, after the auctioneer calls for bids on an article or lot, that article or lot cannot be withdrawn unless no bid is made within a reasonable time.
Reserve Price: A confidential minimum amount set by the seller. The lot will not be sold unless a bid meets or exceeds this threshold.
Fall of the Hammer: The customary action or announcement by the auctioneer that legally completes a sale by auction and creates a binding contract.
Bid Increment: The predetermined step amount by which each successive new bid must exceed the previously acknowledged bid.
Timed Listing: Offering goods for sale online with a fixed ending time and date that does not extend based on bidding activity.

Soft Close: A feature in online or hybrid auctions that briefly extends the auction's end time by a set number of minutes after a late bid is placed, preventing "sniping" and encouraging competitive bidding.

sections.heading: Legal & Ethical Concepts
content: Commingling: The illegal practice of mixing funds belonging to clients (like earnest money or seller proceeds) with an auctioneer's personal or business operating funds.
Escrow / Trustee Account: A dedicated bank account that principal auctioneers must maintain to strictly hold funds belonging to others coming into their possession as a result of a sale.
Cappers / Pullers: False, fake, or "shill" bidders knowingly used by an auctioneer to artificially inflate the bidding price or stimulate market momentum.
Memorandum of Sale: A written document that provides formal proof of the contract between the buyer and seller, satisfying the Statute of Frauds (especially for the sale of goods over $500).

Bulk Sales Laws: Statutes intended to protect creditors by requiring a seller to notify them (and sometimes file a formal statement) before disposing of a substantial portion, or all, of a business's inventory at auction.

Engaged in the Business (Firearms): A federal ATF standard dictating who needs a Federal Firearms License (FFL). It applies to a person who devotes time, attention, and labor to dealing in firearms as a regular course of trade or business to predominantly earn a profit.

sections.heading: Auction Math & Financial Terms
content: Hammer Price: The final accepted bid amount on an item before adding any buyer's premiums or applicable taxes.
Buyer's Premium: A predetermined percentage added to the final hammer price. The buyer's premium is always considered part of the "Sale Price" for the purpose of calculating sales tax.
Capitalization Rate: A real estate evaluation metric representing the rate of return on an investment property, calculated by dividing the annual income by the property's value.
Appraised Value: The estimated total market value of a real estate property before tax rates are applied.

Assessed Value: The value of a property used for tax purposes. It is calculated by multiplying the Appraised Value by the property's specific statutory Tax Rate (e.g., 25% for residential, 40% for commercial).
Mil Rate: The Assessed Value divided by 100. This figure is then multiplied by the specific county or city tax rate to determine the final property taxes owed.
Net Income: The final profit calculated by subtracting operating expenses from the gross income.

sections.heading: Module 1: Licensing Requirements & Administration
content: Affiliate Auctioneer: Must be at least 18 years old, complete a 50-hour approved auction education program, pass the affiliate examination, and be sponsored by a principal auctioneer.

Principal Auctioneer: Must be at least 18, have obtained a high school diploma or GED, serve as an affiliate under a principal auctioneer for at least 6 months, obtain a minimum of 1,000 points (the equivalent of working 5 complete auctions), and pass the principal auctioneer exam.
Public Automobile Auctioneer: Must be at least 18, have served as a principal auctioneer for 2 years, and complete 15 hours of rigorous classroom/online instruction in automobile auctioneering.
Bid Caller: Must be at least 18 and complete a 16-hour course on the basic fundamentals of auctioneering.

Continuing Education (CE): Licensees must complete 6 hours of continuing education every two years to renew their license. Licensees must personally maintain proof of completion of each continuing education course for two renewal cycles (four years).

Exemptions from Licensure: Licensure is not required for trustees in bankruptcy, guardians, those acting under a court order, charitable organizations (if the individual is unpaid), individuals selling their own personal property not purchased for resale, or timed online listings that do not simulcast a live auction.

sections.heading: Module 2: Business Operations, Escrow, & Ethics
content: Escrow Accounts: All principal and public automobile auctioneers must maintain—or have written authorization to access—an escrow or trustee account for all funds belonging to others coming into their possession.
Commingling Prohibited: Mixing client funds with personal or business operating funds, or failing to account for and remit client money within a reasonable time, is grounds for license revocation.

Written Contracts: An auctioneer must enter into a written contract with the owner or consignee of any property detailing the terms and conditions prior to the sale. Copies of written instruments must be furnished voluntarily to all interested parties at the time of execution.

Advertising Rules: All advertising must be in the name and license number of the principal auctioneer responsible for the sale. Advertisements placed by an affiliate auctioneer must conspicuously identify them as an affiliate, along with their license number and the name and license number of their sponsor.
False Bidders: Knowingly using false bidders, cappers, or pullers is strictly prohibited and is grounds for license suspension or revocation.

sections.heading: Module 3: Disciplinary Actions & The Recovery Fund
content: The Citation Process: The administrative director or investigator can issue citations for violations. Penalties range from $50 to $2,500 per violation. An individual has 15 working days to appeal a citation.
Auctioneer Education & Recovery Account: Established to compensate aggrieved consumers who have won judgments against licensees for fraud or violations.
The state must maintain a minimum balance of $150,000 in this account.
Maximum payout liability is 20,000 per licensee.
If a claim is paid out on behalf of an auctioneer, their license may be suspended or revoked. They are ineligible to apply for a new license until they repay the account in full, plus interest.
Licensees acting in their professional capacity and bonding companies have no claim against the account.
Module 4: Auction Law & The Uniform Commercial Code (UCC Section 2-328)
Lot Sales: If goods are put up in lots, each lot is the subject of a separate sale.
Fall of the Hammer: A sale by auction is complete when the auctioneer announces it by the fall of the hammer.
Rule: If a bid is made while the hammer is falling in acceptance of a prior bid, the auctioneer may, in their discretion, reopen the bidding or declare the goods sold under the prior bid.
With Reserve (Default): An auction is "with reserve" unless explicitly stated otherwise. The auctioneer may withdraw the goods at any time until announcing completion of the sale.
Without Reserve (Absolute): After the auctioneer calls for bids on an article, it cannot be withdrawn unless no bid is made within a reasonable time.
Bid Retraction: A bidder may retract their bid until the completion of the sale, but a retraction does not revive any previous bid.

Seller Bidding: If the auctioneer knowingly receives an undisclosed bid on the seller's behalf, the buyer may choose to avoid the sale or take the goods at the price of the last good-faith bid (Note: this does not apply to forced sales).
Module 5: Specialized Auctions & Federal Regulations

Real Estate Auctions: To conduct a real estate auction, you must hold both an auctioneer/affiliate license AND a broker/affiliate broker license. If an affiliate auctioneer is calling the bids, their employing auctioneer must be physically present on the premises.

Automobile Auctions: A public automobile auction must hold a valid motor vehicle dealer license and an auctioneer license. They must maintain a $500,000 garagekeeper's legal liability insurance policy and a $50,000 surety bond. The auction must take possession of the vehicle, and the seller must sign the title over to the public automobile auction, who then signs it over to the buyer.
Federal Firearms License (FFL): Required if you repetitively buy and sell firearms to predominantly earn a profit.

An auctioneer does not need an FFL if they simply offer bid-calling services (e.g., traveling to an estate) where the firearms remain the property of the estate until legally transferred directly to the buyer.
However, if you take consignment of firearms at your auction house to make a profit from those specific sales, you do need an FFL.
IRS Form 8300 (Cash Transactions): You must file Form 8300 if you receive more than $10,000 in cash in a single transaction or related transactions.
Deadline: Must be filed within 15 days of the transaction.
Customer Notice: You must provide a written statement to the customer named on the form by January 31 of the following year.
E-Filing: Mandatory if your business files 10 or more other information returns (like W-2s or 1099s).

sections.heading: Module 6: Auction Math & Formulas
content: You will need to know standard formulas to solve for variables like property value, interest, taxes, and buyer's premiums.

sections.heading: Rate = Income ÷ Value
content: Interest: Interest Owed = Principal × Interest Rate (for an annual figure). (If paying off early, e.g., 6 months, divide the annual interest by 2).
Principal Loan Amount: Interest ÷ Interest Rate = Principal.

sections.heading: Property Taxes:
content: Assessed Value = Appraised Value × Tax Rate (e.g., Residential 25% or Commercial 40%).
Mil Rate = Assessed Value ÷ 100.
Tax Owed = Mil Rate × County/City Tax Rate.

sections.heading: Sales Tax & Breakouts:
content: Sale Price = Hammer Price + Buyer's Premium.
(Note: The Buyer's Premium is ALWAYS considered part of the sale price for tax purposes).
Sales Tax Due = Sale Price × Tax Rate.

Ticket Total Breakout: To find the Sale Price when given a final inclusive ticket total, use: Sale Price = Ticket Total ÷ (1 + Tax Rate as a decimal). (e.g., Ticket Total of $1204.50 with a 9.5% tax rate = $1204.50 ÷ 1.095 = $1100.00 Sale Price).
